How Should You Install Software on a Mac? Homebrew vs Official Download vs curl

If you've just started using a Mac for development, you've probably noticed something: the same tool has several different install paths. Take Git — some say Xcode Command Line Tools is enough, others recommend Homebrew. Take Node.js — some say download from the official site, some say use nvm, some say use Homebrew.
This post sorts through the common Mac install methods and pairs them with concrete examples so you know which one fits which situation.
Before going through the various install methods, let me cover why most tutorials default to Homebrew.
Homebrew is the most-used package manager on macOS. If you haven't installed it yet, you can follow my older post
Make your Mac terminal pretty — the only guide you need (article in Mandarin)
. In short, it lets you manage software with terminal commands. What makes it better than just downloading from official sites?
Manage all your software in one place
Whether you install command-line tools or GUI apps through Homebrew, you can manage all of them with commands:

# See what you've got installed
brew list

# Update everything at once
brew upgrade

# Fully remove something (no leftover files)
brew uninstall wget

# Clean up old versions taking up space
brew cleanup
# Clean up old versions taking up space

If you download tools from official sites instead, updates mean going back to those sites manually, and uninstalling means hunting down leftover files. After 20–30 tools, it gets really hard to manage.
Some tools require other tools to be installed first — like a package that needs
. If you're installing by hand, you have to track down each dependency yourself. Homebrew handles them automatically — whatever's missing, it pulls in.
Use Brewfile to back up and restore your whole machine
This is one of Homebrew's most useful features. You can export everything you've installed via Homebrew as a

# Export your current software list
brew bundle dump

# Install everything on a new machine in one go
brew bundle install
# Install everything on a new machine in one go
So when you switch to a new Mac or want to sync dev environments with a colleague, share the
and they're one command away from having the same setup. You can't do that by downloading from individual sites.
Homebrew has two install modes for two kinds of software:

# Formulae: command-line tools
brew install git
brew install wget
brew install jq

# Cask: GUI applications
brew install --cask visual-studio-code
brew install --cask google-chrome
brew install --cask docker
installs GUI apps. Using Cask gives you the same end result as downloading a
, with the bonus that you can run
to update everything in one shot and
Beyond Homebrew, there are a few other approaches, each with their place.
If you're not doing iOS development, you don't need full Xcode —
is enough. It includes Git, make, clang, and the basic dev tools:
This is usually the first command you run on a new Mac, since many tools (Homebrew included) depend on it.

Going to the software's official site and downloading the installer. Simple and direct, but updates are on you, and uninstalls don't always clean up — some software scatters config files around the system.
If the same software is available via Homebrew, prefer Homebrew. Much easier to manage.
This pattern usually shows up when the tool needs to modify your shell config (like
), which isn't a great fit for Homebrew's standard flow. Homebrew itself installs via curl.
Before running a curl script, make sure the source is the official repo. You're literally executing code from the internet on your machine.
If you're maintaining multiple projects that each need different language versions (project A on Node.js 18, project B on Node.js 20), you want a version manager — not a plain Homebrew install.
can manage Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, Java, etc., all at once — so you don't have to install a separate version manager per language.
If you want to see how nvm works in practice, I wrote about it in
Auto-switch Node.js version with nvm use when opening a project on Mac (article in Mandarin)
Now let's walk through a few common tools and the install method that fits each.
Git comes with Xcode Command Line Tools, but the bundled version is usually older. If you want a newer version, just use Homebrew:
The Homebrew version auto-overrides the Xcode CLT one. Updates are just
: Xcode CLT is fine for most users. If you care about version, use Homebrew.
Node.js is the tool people get confused about the most:

# Homebrew (only one version possible)
brew install node

# nvm (multiple versions, free switching)
nvm install 20
nvm use 20

# mise (general-purpose version manager)
mise use node@20
from the official site isn't great because it installs at a system-level path, which makes switching versions or uninstalling painful.
: Frontend or Node.js devs should use nvm or mise. If you only touch Node.js occasionally, Homebrew is enough.
Mac ships with Python, but it's an old version and not great for dev:

# Homebrew (installs a new version)
brew install python

# pyenv (multi-version)
brew install pyenv
pyenv install 3.12
pyenv global 3.12

# mise
mise use python@3.12
: If you write Python regularly, use pyenv or mise. If something else just needs Python as a dependency, Homebrew is fine.
VS Code is a GUI app — both methods get you the same result:
: If you're already on Homebrew, use Cask for consistency.
My personal new-Mac install order looks something like this:
GUI apps via Homebrew Cask or official download depending on the situation
You don't have to memorize every method. The rule of thumb is: if Homebrew can manage it, use Homebrew; if you need multiple versions, use a version manager. That alone covers most decisions.

If you've just started using a Mac for development, you've probably noticed there are several different ways to install the same tool — download a `.dmg` from the official site, install with Homebrew, run a `curl` install script, and tutorials sometimes recommend different approaches for the same tool. This post sorts through the common Mac install methods, pairs them with real examples (Git, Node.js, Python, VS Code), so you know which one to pick when.
